meta.topic: Linking verbs: be, appear, seem; become, get, etc.

step_1_context.text: When an adjective or noun phrase is used after a verb to describe the subject or say what or who the subject is, the adjective or noun phrase is a complement and the verb is a linking verb:

step_2_explanation.simple_rule:
When an adjective or noun phrase is used after a verb to describe the subject or say what or who the subject is, the adjective or noun phrase is a complement and the verb is a linking verb:
 • Clara is a doctor.
step_2_explanation.formula:
After the verbs appear (= seems true), look (= seem), prove, seem, and turn out we can often either include or omit to be:
 • The room appears (to be) brighter than when I last saw it.
step_2_explanation.common_mistake: We use the linking verb become to describe a process of change.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 21.1.1 The job turned out to be far easier than I'd expected.
options: turned out (to be) far easier — 'to be' can be omitted
step_3_interactive_exercises.correct_answer: turned out (to be) far easier — 'to be' can be omitted
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: After 'turn out', 'to be' can often be left out before an adjective.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 21.1.2 When I looked through the window, Ella appeared to be alone.
options: appeared to be alone — 'to be' should be KEPT (adjective 'alone')
step_3_interactive_exercises.correct_answer: appeared to be alone — 'to be' should be KEPT (adjective 'alone')
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Before 'alone', 'to be' is usually included after 'appear'.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 21.1.3 What he called his 'little cottage in the country' proved to be a castle.
options: proved to be a castle — 'to be' should be KEPT (noun = what the subject is)
proved (to be) a castle — can omit
step_3_interactive_exercises.correct_answer: proved to be a castle — 'to be' should be KEPT (noun = what the subject is)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Before a noun telling us what the subject IS, include 'to be'.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 21.1.4 Hassan proved to be an excellent source of information about the town.
options: proved (to be) an excellent source — 'to be' can be left out (opinion)
step_3_interactive_exercises.correct_answer: proved (to be) an excellent source — 'to be' can be left out (opinion)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Giving an opinion about the person — 'to be' optional; also in formal English leave it out.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 21.1.6 I've adjusted the aerial and the television seems to be working okay now.
options: seems to be working — 'to be' must be KEPT (before -ing form)
step_3_interactive_exercises.correct_answer: seems to be working — 'to be' must be KEPT (before -ing form)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Before -ing forms of verbs, 'to be' is usually included.
